summary: High‑energy Worcester show with stellar Stranger, Estimated Prophet and Touch of Grey
blurb: The band delivers a tight, high‑octane set highlighted by Feel Like A Stranger, Estimated Prophet and a powerful Touch of Grey opener. Brent’s keyboards sparkle on the second‑set suite, while Row Jimmy and BRRB shine in the matrix capture.
review: April 7 1988 at The Centrum in Worcester captured one of the Dead’s late‑80s high‑energy peaks. Opening with a bright Touch of Grey, the first set rolls through Feel Like A Stranger (Jerry’s voice a little hoarse but his guitar soaring) into Franklin's Tower and a rousing New Minglewood Blues before launching into Row Jimmy and the marathon Stuck Inside of Mobile… that segues straight into Big Railroad Blues and Around & Around. The second set is a showcase: Sugar Magnolia bursts out, then the classic Scarlet Begonias → Estimated Prophet → Eyes Of The World sequence drives the crowd wild, capped by a tight Drum/Space/​Wheel segment that leads into All Along The Watchtower and a fiery Black Peter before closing with Sunshine Daydream. The encore Box of Rain provides a gentle finish. Brent Mydland’s synth work fuels the Estimated Prophet, his keyboards add sparkle throughout, while Bob Weir is on fire in the latter half of Stranger and delivers inventive falsetto on Sugar Magnolia. Phil Lesh holds down a steady, supportive bass line, and Mickey Hart & Bill Kreutzmann keep the rhythmic engine humming with dynamic pushes that power the seamless segues. The Charlie Miller‑remastered soundboard (gd1988-04-07.sbd.miller.91163.sbeok.flac16) captures this performance with crisp clarity, balanced instrument separation, and just enough crowd ambience to convey the miserable weather‑soaked night without distraction.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Five of six recordings are marked positive and one mixed; reviewers consistently praise the high‑energy playing, standout songs (Stranger, Estimated Prophet, Touch of Grey) and tight transitions, with no recurring negative comments about performance quality.

best_recording.reason: Charlie Miller’s remaster provides excellent soundboard clarity, full‑show coverage and no dropouts, capturing the band’s high‑energy performance in crisp detail.

key_highlights: High‑octane set with standout Feel Like A Stranger and Touch of Grey opening
Brent Mydland’s keyboards drive an exceptional Estimated Prophet
Row Jimmy and BRRB moments shine in the matrix capture
Seamless segues through Scarlet Begonias → Estimated Prophet → Eyes Of The World

band_performance.Jerry: Voice is hoarse early on due to a cold but his guitar remains joyous and muscular, especially on Sunshine Daydream; overall he delivers solid, energetic playing.
band_performance.Phil: Phil’s bass stays steady and supportive, anchoring the high‑energy set without any noted lapses.
band_performance.Bob: Bob Weir is on fire, delivering inventive, forceful guitar lines and striking falsetto vocals, particularly on Stranger and Sugar Magnolia.
band_performance.Brent: Brent shines, driving Estimated Prophet with sparkling keyboards; his synth drops out early in Stranger but otherwise adds sparkle throughout the set.
band_performance.Mickey: Mickey Hart contributes tight, dynamic drumming that fuels the powerful segues and second‑set momentum.
band_performance.Billy: Bill Kreutzmann’s steady groove pairs with Mickey to keep the rhythmic engine humming, powering the show’s relentless drive.
